skill.name: context-fundamentals
skill.description: Understand what context is, why it matters, and the anatomy of context in agent systems
skill.summary: Learn context engineering fundamentals including attention budget, progressive disclosure, and context optimization for AI agents
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 34 patterns but all are false positives. The file contains only educational markdown documentation about context engineering with no executable code. External command patterns are markdown code examples, the URL is a source reference in metadata, and cryptographic patterns are false matches on words like attention and token.

content.user_title: Master context engineering fundamentals for AI agents
content.value_statement: AI agents struggle with context management, leading to degraded performance and wasted tokens. This skill teaches the core principles of context engineering including attention budget, progressive disclosure, and token optimization.
actual_capabilities: Explains the five components of context: system prompts, tool definitions, retrieved documents, message history, and tool outputs
Teaches attention budget constraints and how model performance degrades with context length
Demonstrates progressive disclosure technique for loading information on demand
Provides guidelines for organizing system prompts with XML tags or markdown sections
Shows context budgeting strategies including compaction triggers at 70-80 percent utilization
Covers hybrid strategies combining pre-loaded context with just-in-time loading
limitations: This skill provides conceptual knowledge only and does not include executable code implementations
Context optimization techniques require manual implementation in your agent architecture
Specific attention patterns may vary between different language models and versions
Token cost calculations depend on current pricing which changes over time

use_cases.title: Agent Architecture Design
use_cases.description: Use these fundamentals when designing new AI agent systems to establish efficient context management from the start
use_cases.target_user: AI engineers building production agent systems

use_cases.title: Debugging Agent Behavior
use_cases.description: Apply context principles to diagnose unexpected agent responses caused by context overload or poor information placement

use_cases.title: Team Onboarding
use_cases.description: Train new team members on context engineering concepts before they begin working with agent development
use_cases.target_user: Technical leads managing AI development teams

prompt_templates.title: Basic System Prompt Organization
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a system prompt for a coding assistant using XML sections for background information, instructions, tool guidance, and output description. The agent should write Python code following PEP 8 with type hints.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt structure for new developers

prompt_templates.title: Progressive Disclosure Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a context loading strategy for a documentation assistant that starts with file paths and metadata, then loads full content only when the user asks about specific topics.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate context management for documentation tasks

prompt_templates.title: Context Budget Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this agent conversation log and identify opportunities to reduce context usage through observation masking, history compaction, and selective tool result retention while maintaining task performance.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced optimization for long-running agent sessions

prompt_templates.title: Attention Position Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Restructure this 5000 token context to place the most critical instructions at attention-favored positions (beginning and end) while moving reference materials to the middle sections.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt engineering for maximum instruction adherence

output_examples.input: How should I organize my system prompt for a customer support agent?
output: Use XML sections to create clear boundaries:
BACKGROUND_INFORMATION: Company details, product info, support tier
INSTRUCTIONS: Response tone, escalation criteria, personalization rules
TOOL_GUIDANCE: When to search knowledge base vs create tickets
OUTPUT_DESCRIPTION: Response format, required fields, follow-up questions

output_examples.input: My agent forgets instructions in long conversations. How can I fix this?
output: This is context degradation from attention budget depletion. Solutions:
1. Move critical instructions to the very beginning of context
2. Implement history compaction at 70 percent context utilization
3. Use periodic summarization to preserve key state in fewer tokens
4. Design triggers to reload core instructions when context grows large

best_practices: Treat context as a finite resource with diminishing returns as token count increases
Place critical information at attention-favored positions at the beginning and end of context
Implement compaction triggers at 70-80 percent context utilization before degradation occurs
anti_patterns: Loading all available documentation upfront instead of using progressive disclosure
Assuming larger context windows solve memory problems without considering attention degradation
Placing important instructions in the middle of long contexts where attention is weakest

faq.question: What is the attention budget in language models?
faq.answer: The attention budget refers to the finite computational capacity models have for processing token relationships. As context length increases, the model must spread its attention across more token pairs, reducing precision for any single relationship and degrading performance on complex reasoning tasks.

faq.question: How do I know when to implement context compaction?
faq.answer: Monitor context usage during development and set compaction triggers at 70-80 percent of your model effective context limit. This provides a safety margin before performance degradation affects agent behavior. Track metrics like instruction adherence and task completion rates as context grows.

faq.question: What is progressive disclosure and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Progressive disclosure loads information only when needed rather than pre-loading everything. Start with lightweight references like file paths or summaries, then load full content dynamically when the task requires it. This keeps initial context small while maintaining access to detailed information on demand.

faq.question: Does a 200K context window mean I can put everything in context?
faq.answer: No. While models technically support large windows, performance degrades significantly before hitting the limit. Research shows reduced precision for information retrieval and reasoning in long contexts. Focus on context quality and relevance rather than maximizing token count.

faq.question: What are the five components of context?
faq.answer: System prompts define agent identity and constraints. Tool definitions specify available actions. Retrieved documents provide domain knowledge. Message history contains the conversation. Tool outputs are results from agent actions. Each component has different characteristics and optimization strategies.

faq.question: How can I reduce token costs while maintaining agent performance?
faq.answer: Use observation masking to hide irrelevant tool outputs, compact conversation history through summarization, implement selective retention keeping only relevant results, and apply progressive disclosure to avoid loading unnecessary information. Focus on informativity over exhaustiveness.
